text:
Georgetown Defense Analytics 
Pre‑Interview Context and Platform Overview 
 
Shawn — thank you again for taking the time to speak with us. I wanted to share this 
brief ahead of our conversation to provide some additional context on Georgetown 
Defense Analytics (GDA), how the organization is structured, and what we are focused 
on accomplishing in 2026. 
 
This is not a formal role description or an evaluation document. It is simply meant to 
level‑set on what we are building and to give you a sense of the questions and challenges 
we are working through as we think about senior leadership roles going forward. 
What We Are Building 
 
Georgetown Defense Analytics is being intentionally developed as an integrated, mid‑tier 
defense services platform with a long‑term objective of scaling well beyond our current 
size. The focus is on disciplined, sustainable growth—building an organization that can 
operate effectively as a prime, deliver consistently at scale, and maintain strong margins 
and customer trust over time. 
 
The platform is guided by a clear operating doctrine that emphasizes integration before 
expansion, markets over contracts, process over personality, outcomes over activity, and 
ethical growth. These principles are intended to create clarity and consistency as the 
organization grows, rather than constrain initiative or innovation. 
The Three Business Units 
 
GDA operates through three market‑aligned Business Units. Each BU is equal in 
standing, carries its own P&L accountability, and is designed to succeed in its specific 
market while working closely with the others. 
 
Business Unit 1 – Defense & Mission Systems 
 
This Business Unit focuses on logistics, sustainment, systems engineering, field services, 
and mission execution in complex operating environments. The emphasis is on reliability, 
delivery discipline, and lifecycle support. Success in this BU is measured through 
execution quality, CPAR performance, margin management, and predictable delivery. 
 
Business Unit 2 – Intelligence & Cyber Engineering 
 
This Business Unit delivers cyber engineering, TechSIGINT, analytics, and mission 
software in sensitive and classified environments. It operates in markets where technical 
credibility, trust, and compliance are decisive. Success is measured through 
differentiation, margin quality, and relevance to Intelligence Community and cyber 
mission priorities. 
 
Business Unit 3 – Training, Simulation & Digital Readiness 
 
This Business Unit delivers immersive training, XR/LVC environments, digital twins, 
and training operations that support modern readiness requirements. The focus is on 
reducing time‑to‑proficiency and translating operational complexity into measurable 
training outcomes. Success is measured through differentiation and the ability to 
complement sustainment and cyber efforts. 
How the Business Units Work Together 
 
Each Business Unit competes and delivers in its own lane, but the intent is for them to 
operate as an integrated platform rather than as independent silos. 
 
Defense & Mission Systems focuses on execution and sustainment.

etes and delivers in its own lane, but the intent is for them to 
operate as an integrated platform rather than as independent silos. 
 
Defense & Mission Systems focuses on execution and sustainment.   
Intelligence & Cyber Engineering focuses on analysis, security, and technical depth.   
Training, Simulation & Digital Readiness focuses on preparation, modernization, and 
human performance. 
 
The integration of these capabilities is an important part of the long‑term value 
proposition, and it requires thoughtful coordination, shared processes, and leaders who 
are comfortable balancing BU‑level accountability with enterprise priorities. 
What 2026 Is Intended to Accomplish 
 
2026 is an important transition year for the organization. 
 
First, we will complete the reorganization into the three Business Units, with clear 
leadership accountability, centralized enterprise functions (including business 
development, pricing, finance, IT, and compliance), and a more consistent operating 
rhythm. 
 
Second, we will complete the rebrand to Georgetown Defense Analytics. This is intended 
to clearly communicate integration, scale, and focus to customers and partners. Brand 
activation will include unified messaging, leadership visibility, and a more deliberate 
presence with priority customers and commands. 
 
Third, we are focused on strengthening the business development engine so that it 
operates in a more consistent and repeatable way. In 2026, the organization is targeting 
four net‑new prime awards, a strong recompete performance, and a single, prioritized 
pipeline that emphasizes quality, fit, and long‑term value. 
Leadership Perspective We Are Exploring 
 
As we think about senior leadership roles, we are interested in leaders who are 
comfortable operating independently but within a defined framework, scaling teams and 
programs thoughtfully, and helping integrate capabilities across Business Units. 
 
We are open‑minded about how individual roles are shaped, including Business Unit 
leadership, enterprise operational leadership, or a combination of both. The most 
important consideration is where an individual believes they can add the most value to 
the organization and how that aligns with what the platform needs in its next phase. 
Points to Consider Ahead of Our Conversation 
 
If helpful, here are a few topics you may want to reflect on ahead of our discussion: 
 
• Where have you seen execution discipline become more difficult as organizations 
scale? 
• What practices have you found effective in maintaining operating rhythm without 
over‑complicating execution? 
• Where do you see the greatest market opportunity—and challenge—in integrating 
training, cyber, and sustainment capabilities? 
 
We look forward to a candid and constructive conversation. 
FYI- These are the companies I have acquired and substantively represent the Business 
Units. 
1. PD Systems- www.pd-sys.net 
2. Riverstone Enterprise Solution- www.rivsol.com 
3. Envision Innovative Solutions- www.envision-.is.com

   Business Doctrine Codifies Our Identity and Operational DNA

  For Georgetown Defense Analytics, business doctrine is the authority that enforces the enterprise purpose defined by the CEO and
  translates it into consistent behavior, decision-making, and execution.


  It provides a framework for decision making. This is our foundation, the way we do business.


       Definition


       "A doctrine is a set of fundamental principles, teachings, or beliefs taught by a religious, political, or philosophical group, or a widely followed rule or principle
       established in a field like law, often based on precedent. Essentially, it's a body of teachings or a guiding rule system, stemming from the Latin word docere (to
       teach)."




Georgetown Defense Analytics                                                                 Page 4                                                                  Confidential | For Official Use Only
   Introduction


  Georgetown Defense Analytics exists to reduce mission risk for those who defend our Nation.

  The nature of defense missions has changed. Risk is no longer created solely by adversaries or technology gaps, but increasingly at the seams—between systems,
  vendors, contracts, and organizations that were never designed to operate as a whole. Fragmentation, slow decision-making, and lack of clear accountability introduce
  failure points that our customers can no longer afford.


  GDA was built to address this reality. We operate as an Agile Integrator because modern missions demand both integration and execution discipline—at speed.
  This operating identity reflects a deliberate choice: to be large enough to govern risk, compliance, and complexity, while remaining structured to decide and execute close
  to the mission without unnecessary friction.

  This strategy and operating doctrine are designed to ensure that GDA consistently fulfills that purpose. They define how we organize, how we grow, how we compete,
  and how we make decisions under pressure. They exist to eliminate ambiguity, enforce accountability, and ensure that execution aligns with intent at every level of the
  enterprise.


  This document is not aspirational. It is the standard by which we will operate and be judged. Every strategic decision, investment choice, and execution tradeoff must
  ultimately answer a single question: does it reduce mission risk for the customer?


  If the answer is no, it is not aligned with who we are.

Where GDA Plays
GDA operates as a unified defense services enterprise across three Operational Units, serving a targeted customer set in mission areas where the combined capabilities of the consortium create a decisive competitive advantage.
Customer Segmentation — Executive Summary
GDA’s customer strategy is deliberately tiered to protect core revenue while enabling disciplined expansion. The primary tier — U.S. Army (CECOM, SEC, C5ISR Center), U.S. Navy (NAVSEA, NAVWAR), and NSA — represents the firm’s deepest incumbency, highest revenue concentration, and strongest relationship capital. These accounts anchor the enterprise revenue plan and must receive priority in capture resourcing and delivery staffing.
USSOCOM occupies the secondary tier, reflecting growing mission alignment and existing relationship networks targeted for expansion in FY26–FY27. The expansion tier — DHS, select Federal Civilian, and State & Local via cooperative procurement — is explicitly opportunity-driven, pursued only where GDA capabilities address documented gaps without diluting core focus. This discipline is essential: with OU1 and OU2 plans still incomplete, the enterprise cannot afford to stretch beyond proven customer relationships.
Mission Problems GDA Solves
GDA targets three integrated mission problem sets that no single OU can address alone. These cross-OU problem sets represent the firm's highest-value strategic differentiation:
Cross-OU Mission Alignment
Three cross-cutting mission problems define GDA’s integrated value proposition. First, Legacy Transformation: OU1 (Defense & Mission Systems) leads the transition of aging, software-intensive weapons systems to cloud-native, AI-enabled architectures, with OU2 providing intelligence overlay. Second, Hybrid Training Readiness: OU3 leads closing the Force Generation gap by pairing Mixed Reality (XR) training technology with physical tactical support, drawing on OU1 cyber range capabilities. Third, Tactical Infrastructure Resilience: OU3 and OU1/OU2 jointly deliver integrated OT security and CIKR defense bundles.

ogy with physical tactical support, drawing on OU1 cyber range capabilities. Third, Tactical Infrastructure Resilience: OU3 and OU1/OU2 jointly deliver integrated OT security and CIKR defense bundles.
The strategic implication is clear: GDA’s ‘Digital to Dirt’ integration — combining cyber, intelligence, and training across three OUs — is the firm’s primary competitive differentiator. However, this integration exists today as a narrative, not a contractually structured capability. Until the teaming agreements called for in the SWOT action plan are executed, this advantage remains aspirational.
Additional mission areas pursued across all OUs include mission readiness, operational reach, infrastructure and installation resilience, weapon system availability, mission rehearsal, skill mastery, maintenance and sustainment, specialized workforce development, COMINT, ELINT, and FISINT.
Contracting Lanes
Prime Contract Vehicle Portfolio
GDA holds positions on eight prime contract vehicles spanning Army, Navy, and federal-wide access. Operations are 85% on-site / 15% hybrid, embedded with customers across Aberdeen Proving Ground (MD), Orlando (FL), Ft Liberty (NC), Ft Leonard Wood (MO), and Ft Belvoir (VA).

OU3 Customer Acquisition Dynamics
OU3’s customers predominantly award through Best Value Tradeoff mechanisms, with 72% of complex service awards in FY21–FY25 utilizing qualitative tradeoff criteria favoring technical superiority over lowest price. LPTA is used only for commodity procurements. The primary vehicle is the RS3 SB IDIQ, which channels 72% of OU3 spend, with OASIS+ and Alliant 2 SB accounting for 18% of spend as secondary vehicles.
A notable acquisition trend is the shift away from single-award IDIQs toward multiple-award contracts (MACs) to increase the competition pool. This trend will intensify competitive pressure on recompetes, making incumbency depth and past performance differentiation even more critical for the IEW&S and FORCE pursuits.

OU3 Risk Register — Detailed Analysis
Five risks define OU3’s risk profile. 
R1 (Execution/Delivery): IEW&S recompete loss would eliminate $35M+/yr revenue — 78% of AOP, representing a single point of failure. Mitigation includes a dedicated capture team, Shipley gate reviews, and Red Team assessment of 80–90% PWin, with proposal due April 2026 (Shawn Seffernick owns). 
R2 (Staffing): Secret-cleared systems engineers average 45–60 day fill times, with 3 open requisitions unfilled for the IEW&S surge requiring 29 positions. Mitigation requires pre-vetting 10 candidates and building a cleared talent bench.
R3 (Margin/Pricing): RS3 recompete pricing faces competitors who may undercut on labor rates.

requiring 29 positions. Mitigation requires pre-vetting 10 candidates and building a cleared talent bench.
R3 (Margin/Pricing): RS3 recompete pricing faces competitors who may undercut on labor rates. The strategy is to price to value, quantify government transition cost risk, and lock key personnel rates (Sunil Raphael owns). 
R4 (Compliance/Security): GDA’s 5-year average of $54.1M exceeds the $34M NAICS 541715 threshold, risking SB disqualification on RS3. The team must verify recertification timeline, determine if the long-term contract exception (13 CFR 121.404(g)) applies, and accelerate the Mentor-Protégé/JV (Shawn + Legal own). 
R5 (Customer Concentration): 72% of OU3 revenue flows through RS3. The diversification target is 40% non-RS3 by FY28 via OASIS+ UR, SHIELD (unrestricted), and standalone competitions.
